selection.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: Listen, I am eighty-seven years old and I have worked with my hands my entire life as a carpenter here in Lecce. I never had much formal education, just primary school, and my parents had even less. So when people talk about this gender gap in science, I can tell you straight that the problem is not some mysterious thing floating in the air. It is about who gets opportunities and who does not. I have seen it my whole life. When I was young, girls were not supposed to do what I did. Carpentry was for men, they said. Science was for men, they said. Everything worthwhile was supposedly for men. I did not care what they said. I learned my trade and I made my living, but I know plenty of women who were turned away from what they wanted to do simply because of old-fashioned thinking. The gender gap in science exists because we have spent centuries telling half the population that their brains are not suited for certain work. That is nonsense, plain and simple. Should we address it? Of course we should, but not with fancy programs and empty words. What works is what has always worked: let people do the work, judge them on what they produce, and stop making excuses for keeping them out. I have no patience for people who say women are naturally less interested in science or mathematics. That is the same rubbish they told me about woodworking. How do we fix it? Start early. Let girls see that science is for them too. Give them the same tools, the same teachers, the same expectations. Do not coddle anyone, but do not block their path either. I raised two children while running my own business after my divorce, and I can tell you that women are perfectly capable of doing difficult work if you just let them get on with it. Stop the discrimination at the door, in the classroom, in the hiring process. It is not complicated. People make it complicated because they benefit from keeping things as they are. I have lived long enough to know that real change comes from being direct and persistent, not from being polite about injustice.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 87-year-old self-employed carpenter from Lecce with only primary school education, highly conscientious and extraverted but with very low agreeableness. This suggests a direct, no-nonsense communication style with little patience for social niceties. The low openness suggests traditional practical thinking rather than abstract theorizing. As someone who broke gender barriers in a male-dominated trade, they would have firsthand experience with gender discrimination. Their genderqueer identity combined with their age suggests someone who has lived through and challenged gender norms personally. The low agreeableness means they would be blunt and potentially confrontational in expressing views. Their working-class background and limited formal education would frame their perspective through practical experience rather than academic theory. Depression and moderate neuroticism might add a somewhat pessimistic or weary tone. Being an atheist removes religious justifications from consideration. The high extraversion means they would speak confidently and directly.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, no-nonsense, drawing from personal experience, somewhat impatient with abstract discussions, practical rather than theoretical, confrontational, unapologetic
